question_text: Let {Xn}n≥1 be a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables each having probability density function f(x) = e−x, x > 0, otherwise. Let X(n) = max{X1, X2, . . . , Xn} for n ≥1. If Z is the random variable to which {X(n)−log n}n≥1 converges in distribution, as n →∞, then the median of Z equals (round off to
solution:
To find the median of Z, we examine the behavior of X(n) and the limiting distribution of Z
as n →∞. Using standard results for the distribution of the maximum of independent
identically distributed random variables, we find that the median of Z is approximately:
Median(Z) ≈0.32.
Thus, the value is 0.32.
Quick Tip
To solve problems involving the maximum of independent random variables, use the
properties of the distribution and the limiting behavior of the sequence.
